text.heading: The Fisherman of Iboko
text.p: Ten yards of the finest white sand separated the fishing village of thatched bamboo and mud huts from the angry Iboko sea. A lonely white gull flew low, parallel across, then dipped itself here and there, desperate for a catch to quell the gnawing hunger wringing deep inside the pit of its stomach. The small bald-headed old man with eyes like burning coal, looked me over from head to toe, spat and sighed.

'I am sorry for your parents, the papa and mama, who gave birth to you'. By the way', he continued, 'let me ask you. Has it ever occurred to you, why a young and inexperienced hawk never ventures into an enemy territory? Answer me. Mr Ade Answer me'.

I kept mute. I uttered not a single word. All my attention was focused on the small old man. Akpan himself left me for a moment. He concentrated his attention on the ground before him. Grinding and gnashing his teeth, he began to draw silly maps of nowhere in particular in the sand with the big toe of his right leg. From his countenance, it was obvious that the old bugger was reeling with anger; and that truly he hated me.
'Go on Akpan', I cajoled. ' I'm sure you have not exhausted your tale?'

Suddenly Akpan stopped drawing his little maps in the sand, as angry waves also began to lap unto the shores, bathing every body present with salty foams. 'Mr Ade', Akpan hissed, reverting back his blood-shot eyes from the sand unto me.

'You small rat. How dare you challenge me?. If you are a stranger in Iboko, why do you not first and foremost find out who is who in this village, before daring me, Akpari Etete Jackson Udoh a.k.a "Tomorrow too far"?
'Akpan', I replied, trying my utmost to re-engage the old man's gaze.
'Since I came into Iboko, I have held a few young ladies by their hands, some by the waist, but I have never touched a married woman. Does that not in a way show that I know who is who?'
'Hmmm —?. I see !', Akpan taunted.

'So you know me?. You know who I am and you have the effrontery to challenge me? . Mr Ade, I swear, I will not forgive you. Nobody in Iboko has ever stood before me to shake a finger, and lived to tell the story, after. Send words home to your father and mother to start making your coffin', the old man yelled.

As the argument between me and Akpan was heating up, those fishermen present, citizens of Iboko, urged me to apologise to Akpan. They persuaded me to apologise and appease him, whether I was right or wrong. Ernestly, they urged that I apologise and beg, to be spared my life. They thought I had been reckless, not to have stayed out of the way of Akpan's wrath. Indeed, the fishermen regarded me with pity, or that sort of feeling one has when one witnesses a helpless child drowning, for example, without one being able to save or help it. They were all on my side though, silently, secretly. But they dared not risk Akpan's wrath, by letting him know that they sided with me in this argument, or even that they sympathised with me.

Etekamba the oldest of the fishermen tried to clear his throat. His first and second attempts were barely audible. It was obvious he was scared and terrified of the venture he had decided to embark upon. His right hand was trembling visibly from where he stood, as he tried to gather his loin-cloth about him. With the fingers of his left hand folded beneath his deeply furrowed chin, his eyes cast on the ground in front of him, Etekamba was a man groaning under an unimaginable yoke. Indeed, his venture seemed most tortuous and beyond the ordinary call of duty. 'Mr Ade', Etekamba began in a very grave high pitched and shaky voice, 'a child carried on the back can never tell how tedious the journey to a distant farm is. The canoe is also said to have opined that; 'nobody would ever enter it, who knows what dangers it sees in the deep'. 'Beg the man to forgive you. Beg him whether you are right or wrong. It is that rat that brags, knows no fear, that is normally found dead in a trap. Look around you at our village Iboko', Etekamba croaked.

Against my will, but at Etekamba's behest, my eyes wandered about, trailing after his gaze, as the old man took a painful swoop over Iboko, or what remained of it, with his experienced, sad and thoughtful gaze. An uncompleted building here. An uncompleted building there. Some of the houses had only reached foundation level. Some without roofs. Some were so weather-beaten that their block walls, door and window frames had even begun to disintegrate and to grow mushroom and fungi. Their owners, one after another, had suddenly died under mysterious circumstances without completing them. Their owners, one and all, had been killed by the powerful witchcraft owned by none other than Akpan Etete Jackson Udoh - (a.k.a -Tomorrow too far). All of them were killed by this wizard of wizards, because, he envied their progress. That was the kind of person Akpan was to his own people, a real terror. So one can imagine why Etekamba and all the other fishermen were asking me to apologise to Akpan, whether I had actually offended him or not. Etekamba was urging me to try to salvage and retrieve my life, from the talons of Akpan's sorcery, and sure death..

Akpan bent down almost double, and picked up a broomstick that lay on the ground in front of him. Slowly, he broke the broom, closed his eyes, and used one end of the broom to remove wax from inside his ears. The silence that engulfed the entire vicinity was horrendous, a silence of fear. No one dared to cough. Indeed, one might even have been able to hear the footsteps of an ant. The suspense in the air was beyond comprehension! After Etekamba had spoken, all the other fishermen sat with their legs folded under their buttocks, eyes glued on the sandy floor before them. No one dared look Akpan in the face. Not a single word had further crossed a lip. Akpan thoroughly enjoyed the state of siege in which he held his people. It was as if even the waves were at Akpan's command, and would break-loose and sweep everybody out to sea, if Akpan so desired.
I said nothing in spite of Etekamba's passionate plea. After a while, Akpan shifted his gaze and rested his burning coal-red eyes on me again.

'Mr em- em -em —. What again do you say is your name? Anyway whatever your name is, I give you four hours to pack your bag and baggage and leave Iboko. Go away in four hours, or send words home to your parents to prepare your coffin'.
'You can't do f---up', I said. It was high time, I thought, to call Akpan's bluff.

'Chei', the crowd shrieked in shock and bewilderment. Those were the very words, I later learnt, Akpan's wife Nkoyo, had uttered to her husband, a few years back, before he used witchcraft to kill her.

Rumour had it, that Nkoyo had quarrelled with her husband one night, five years back. It was said, that Akpan's wife, had pleaded with her husband to desist from his evil ways. The woman was said to have been heard by neighbours pleading with her husband, to change his evil ways, as she was fed up with people referring to her as the wife of a wizard. It was said that Akpan that night had shouted and threatened his wife to stop bothering him, or he would teach her an unforgettable lesson. The woman had lost her head in the heat of such argument, and in desperation had told her husband, 'You can't do f---up'. Those very words that had escaped from my lips moments ago.

Three days after the quarrel, the story continued, Akpan's wife and two other women, her friends, had crossed the Iboko narrow strait, opposite the isthmus that links Iboko with Cross River in a canoe to go and pick periwinkles. The sea was calm that day, very calm indeed, but the canoe had somehow capsized. Without difficulty, the two other women had swum to shore, since the accident had taken place in shallow calm waters. But Akpan's wife had simply disappeared. Two days later, some fishermen had caught a medium sized shark. When they dragged the mammal to shore and opened its belly, they had discovered a human leg inside the animal. That same day a powerful oracle from a neighbouring village had divined, that it was Akpan, who through witchcraft, had overturned the canoe, and caused the shark to swallow Nkoyo, his own wife.

Such was the need to call Akpan's bluff, as you can see. So, in spite of the sweat that had begun to run down my ribs inside my shirt, and the fact that my heart was pounding in my chest like hollow gongs, I tried my best, to look cool and unfazed.

'Akpan', I began, 'it is only a bird that once it learns to fly, flies away and never again remembers mother and father, or a place called home. But a man is never like that. Man always has a home. His own ancestral home. And every ancestral home in Black Africa, as you may know, has an ancestral spirit. Their own family god that guards and guides them. Furthermore', I invited Akpan. 'look around you. Do you see the earth and the sky, the sand, the trees, the sun and the waters of the ocean? .

Every home has the earth and the sky; and in between them are housed whatever ingredient one needs for witchcraft. All these that you have in Iboko, I also have in my own ancestral village called kira. So, if you can kill, I also have all the ingredients in my native village to make witchcraft to kill you too, if I so desire'.
Akpan gnashed his teeth for the last time, then slowly stretched his wrinkled right had to shake me. 'You speak like a real man', he croaked, nodding his small bald head.

'When we started, I thought you were a mere kid, a spoilt child trying to rub shoulders with elders. But I was wrong. Yes! You-speak-like a man', he said, counting the words. 'But Mr Ade, that does not change anything I have told you. In fact, your boldness makes my warning even more urgent, more imperative! Send words home for your coffin. And do so quickly too'.
Those were the very last words Akpan spoke to me, over his shoulders, as he stalked away from the shore, leaving me and the other fishermen behind.

One might wonder what might have brought about my palaver with Akpan. The story is simple and straight forward. I am a civil servant, an extension officer with the local Ministry of Agriculture. I was sent to Iboko some three months or so ago, to assist and organise the peasant fishermen of Iboko into a co-operative society. When I opened the small liaison office here, I employed two staff, a clerk and a messenger. The clerk was Ufot Jackson Udoh, the son of Akpan. A fortnight ago, I was on tour to other stations under my jurisdiction. When I returned, I discovered that Ufot had sacked the messenger, and had employed his old man in his place.
'You can't do that', I had chided Ufot. 'Please ask your father to leave immediately, or I will sack you'. That was all I said.
And that was the reason why I had earned Akpan's venom.

Anyway, the palaver sorted itself out last night. I'm sorry to say that we woke up this morning to hear that Akpan, the old fishermen of Iboko died in his sleep late last night. lt is surprising how these rural people have reacted to the event. I am about to offend so many more people in Iboko, since I do not have the time or stomach to listen to, or receive all the delegations that are billed to call on me today.
